speaker: interviewer
text: We're-- let's go back to Kris--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Freddy, more about Kristallnacht. So it was that German lady upstairs whom your mother was so good to who helped you guys survive through Kristallnacht. Did that-- did that guy upstairs do anything else for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: But w-- let me tell you something. We were so frightened. The children-- we were still all at home. We were hiding on top-- below the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], how do you call it? The-- the-- the-- the-- the roof, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ceiling.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we're holding each other. We was, I mean, scared to death that they will come up and beat us up, because the-- the noise they made and the screaming and the abuses, the language they spoke, that what we expected. And they literally knocked at the door dozens of times, and we were lucky. It was a very strong door. It held up, and-- and thank God that they ran away. And the next morning when daylight came up, we went down. We saw what they did there, and it was frightening. And about on the 10th of November, I would say about 12 o'clock, suddenly the Gestapo came, and they told my parents that the store doesn't belong to us anymore. All the Jewish property is being confiscated. They-- the Nazis gave a punishment to the Jewish population in Germany of a billion marks for causing all the damage. They did the damage, and we were the cause for the damage. And-- and you know that as a result of the Kristallnacht, this young man, Grynszpan, shot the first secretary of the German embassy in Paris, and then they came and arrested all Polish Jews and deport them to Poland. Well, again, it was an action by the Gestapo; whoever was home was caught. My luck was I wasn't home. My father wasn't home. But my two brothers were home. They were arrested and were deported. If you remember [NON-ENGLISH], it's a-- a border town in Poland not far from Poznan. And the-- the Poles didn't want to take them in, and the Germans didn't want to take them. They were in Niemandsland-- in no man's land-- for two or three days, and then thousands were deported to Poland. And my two brothers-- the older brothers-- they were deported to Poland at that time. That was really frightening. And we were just lucky, my father and myself, that-- that that happened a few hours, and that's it. Whoever they caught got caught. We were lucky we're not caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we knew that all the promises that the Nazis made throughout the years that they will destroy the Jews, they will take care of the Jews, that they really did it. My mother was an indestructible optimist. On many occasions when we read in the German newspapers what Hitler predicted, what Goebbels predicted, it just-- it's just politics. The world, the civilized world, is never going to tolerate this. She was such an optimist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the reason why we really didn't make a serious effort to emigrate. I think we had enough means financially. We did very well, because-- I give you an example. Gymnasium and lyceum, you have to pay. It was very expensive in Germany. It's almost like a university here. And her younger brother, in-- in Palestine at that time, made several times phone calls and letters we should sell everything and go into paint, because he married into a family. They had a paint factory in Israel. And-- and IG Farben was famous, world famous, for inventing new paints, new-- new-- let's put it this-- kind of paints that were superior to other paints. And he said, buy yourself paints, and we open up and-- and you join his family in a paint factory. As a matter of fact, he invited us even to meet with his in-laws-- there was a-- a world exposition in Paris at the time, in 1936-- to go there, and to-- to do the details. My mother said, no. With six children, I'm not going to move. I'm not going to move. I stay, and I still believe that God will not permit this to happen. Although he's-- he's predicting and threatening, Hitler is just, you know, a lot of noise. That's all. After the Kristallnacht, she knew that she was wrong, but that was too late. By then it was impossible. All the nations in the world closed their doors. There was the famous Evian Conference where the United States and other nations promised to take in Jewish refugees, but nothing came out of it. It was very, very difficult. Some got out, but-- but most got stuck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is-- and then we lived, so to speak, in limbo until the war broke out on September the 1st. And then a few days later the Gestapo came, and they arrested all the male-- all the male Jews-- foreign Jews. The German Jews they arrested on Kristallnacht, but the Polish Jews were not arrested then. The Polish Jews were arrested when the war with Poland broke out. On Nove-- September the 9th-- the war broke out on September the 1st-- they came and took us-- my-- my father, my brother, myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God, my older brother, I told you-- oh. What I forgot to tell you is when my two brothers were in Poland, through the intervention of the Gestapo-- my mother bribed some of the Gestapo people-- they allowed him to come back for the purpose of emigrating. For example, from '38 to '39, the Nazis actively promoted and supported Jews to get out. Even Eichmann, this murderer, it's known in Vienna that he called on the Jewish community, on the leaders, get them out of here while you can. Get out. Get out. Make every effort. Sell everything. Take every penny and-- and get a visa to get out, anywhere in the world. And people tried to get out. And so my brother-- the oldest brother-- had a-- a-- an official-- how do you say this-- from the underground Jewish Zionist organization to go illegally to Israel. And my younger-- the other brother, who was killed in Auschwitz, also was supposed to get out, but for some reason the message that he should go illegally and meet there, and there, and there got lost somehow. He got stuck with me. It was us together. Now in September the 9th, they arrested only the male Jewish foreign nationals. The women were sent-- I wouldn't call it a ghetto-- in a neighborhood where a lot of Jewish people lived. They had to move out of the house and to move in a neighborhood where all the Jewish citizens, the women and the children, were concentrated, and they lived there until 1942. And then after the infamous Wannsee Conference, they were all deported to the ghetto Riga. All the Jewish people, the women and children-- little children-- were deported to Riga, while we were still in the concentration camp. Now there were cases where people that were in [? concentrate ?]-- by the way, the concentration camp we were sent in was Sachsenhausen near Berlin. It's a very-- one of the oldest concentration camps. As a matter of fact, Sachsenhausen was, so to speak, the mother of all the concentration camps. The general administration of all the concentration camps at that time existed only in Germany. There was no Auschwitz at that time. There was Buchenwald, Dachau, the Mauthausen, and Sachsenhausen. The-- the central administration was also in-- in Sachsenhausen because it was located only 25 kilometers from Berlin. It was very close to the Gestapo headquarters, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Freddy, tell me what happened when they took you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --out of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a frightening experience. First they put us into the local jail for a week or two weeks-- two weeks, probably. It-- it-- the world was-- was-- I mean, it was the end of the world, as far as I'm concerned. I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15. I couldn't stand it to be in a cell all day long. Every minute was like a day, like an hour. It was terrible. We had nothing to eat. We were walking around like an animal in a cage. I remember that. And in a way, it was a kind of, call it, improvement when we went to concentration camp. At least we'd be-- you could walk around. We were in a barrack, and we're together with other Jews. There we were isolated. Each family or each person was in separate cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My fath-- my brother, yeah, in one cell. And it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't give you food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they gave us food, but-- but, I mean, prison food, which was lousy, let's put it this way. That was-- the point was to be isolated like an animal. That was the worst thing. I-- I-- I couldn't take it. I wasn't sleeping at night. I was just thinking, what's going to be with us? It was a very frightening experience because they were pretty rough with us, the-- the jail-- the policemen-- the guards, so to speak. They were very rough with us-- I mean, not outright offensively anti-Semitic, but rough. Rough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you felt any anti-Semitism before this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Let me say this. The gymnasiums and the lyceum, everybody then-- the Germans-- had to be active in the Hitler Youth. And because they were considered, so to speak, the better-educated, because very few people could afford to go to gymnasium in Germany-- Germans at that time were still pretty poor-- they were supposed to be Hitler Youth fuhrers-- in other words, leaders. And some of them were very anti-Semitic. And they, on every occasion they saw me, they-- they knew I was-- that I was Jewish. For some reason, the Germans could always detect. How-- how they did it, I don't know. I looked like anybody else. I didn't particularly look Jewish at all. I had dark hair, yeah. And most of the German Jews were blond, but were also people with black hair. And-- And-- and it--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was-- what were the signs of Jewishness, a sort of universal German sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'm wondering. But they knew. They knew. They recognized. No, no. I-- I would say the vast majority of the Jews didn't have a-- a-- what they called a [GERMAN], a Jewish nose. No, definitely not. Especially German Jews didn't have that. But I don't know. They recognized. Maybe we were softer in our behavior. We were not as aggressive. We were not as arrogant. I mean, those Hitler Youth leaders, they were very arrogant. They really believed what Hitler told them, that they are the master race, that they are destined-- destined to rule the world, and they are superior to anybody else. And they-- they were so brainwashed, they believed it. I mean, you have no idea. They had to go every day to an hour or two to-- to the-- the Nazi headquarters for-- the Nazi Youth headquarters, and they were-- they were brainwashed. They were-- I mean, they were told all this crazy ideology of the Nazis, that they really are supermen and that they really are better than anybody else, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So had you ever had any kind of personal experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The worst part was every morning when the teacher came up they had to stand up and say heil Hitler. You know, before they said good morning, but then it was heil Hitler. And I had to stand up--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it-- I got into-- into school in '33 just when Hitler took over. At the-- it took a few months until the day they instituted all those-- those Hitler adulations, so to speak. And everybody stand up and heil Hitler. And I had to stand up and raise the hand. I didn't say, heil Hitler, but I had to rai-- that was required. If I didn't do it, they-- they would have punished me. They would have thrown me out. The worst part is that they had already then as part of the requirement to-- to learn race theory, and I sit in a class where they teach you that the Jews are the polluters of every race. The Jews are inferior. The Jews are like animals. They are not qualified to be considered human beings. And-- and the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE]. It was a lot of junk.

words.text: heil Hitler, but I had to rai--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was teaching this? The worst, most stupid teacher-- the music teacher, who was an alcoholic, but ran around in a Nazi uniform. He-- to the best of my knowledge, he was the only teacher that ran around in this gymnasium in a Nazi uniform. You remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [GERMAN]. And he was-- he was a drunk. Most of the time he was drunk. And-- and if he wouldn't have been a member of the Nazi party, the-- the-- the-- the rector-- the rector of the school would have thrown him out because he was totally unqualified to be a teacher. He came drunk to-- and he was teaching race theory. They used him because he was a member of the Nazi party. They could trust him. It was just a sham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, we had-- every day we had a sport. It was requirement. The Nazis put a lot of emphasis, the Nazis, on physical education, you can imagine, to create a new German Superman, so to speak. And we-- that I'll never forget. And we never-- we had to learn also boxing. Was requirement, not-- how you call this when-- when you choose it? Not-- what do you say in-- in school? Wrestling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was not a choice, that you could choose.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. It was a requirement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Requirement. And they always gave me a bully that-- I wasn't very tall. I was very underdeveloped. I was very small. And-- and to box him. And they expected him to-- to beat me to-- to-- how do you call it? To--

speaker: interviewer
text: To a pulp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To pulp. I was so quick, he always hit air. [LAUGHS] I was dugging and-- ducking, and I remember it like yesterday.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's go back to-- to the prison. How long were you in the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about two weeks, probably. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, more. It was probably three weeks. And then in October, one day they-- they told us, we are going to send you to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. In October, the beginning of October. We were arrested in September the 9th-- on September the 9th. We were two or three weeks there, and then we were transported-- interesting enough, not in cattle wagons. At that time in Germany, they still pretended to the world that they are civilized and they obey by international rules. They-- they pretended. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sent us to-- to Sachsenhausen. It took us a whole day because there was a-- a special train. We had to wait here and to wait there until they got permission to-- to progress. We arrived at night in this infamous Sachsenhausen camp. And like any concentration camp, the entrance they had 'Arbeit Macht Frei,' you know? Labor makes you free, so to speak. Work makes you free. We marched in. We were about, I would say, 67 men. I was the youngest of them. The oldest was an old man, a-- a yeshiva type-- a very religious Jew with a long beard-- and he must have been early 70s or-- or the very late 60s, which at that time was considered very old. Not like today, when people reach the age of 80. If somebody lived to be 68, 69, it was considered a very old age, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we marched in at the Kommandantur, and we were standing near the-- the gate for almost two, three hours-- standing. That's all. And there was an SS guard that said, don't sit down! Stand up. And some people were weak. There were elderly, there were sick people among us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then finally, who appeared? That's interesting. Who was, so to speak, the guy that gave us greetings or introduce us to the laws and the tradition of the concentration camps? The later kommandant of Auschwitz. His name was Rudolf Hoss. Maybe you heard the name. He was then only a second lieutenant, or in-- in the SS slogan-- jargon, he was an untersturmfuhrer, not even a sturmfuhrer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He comes up, and he was-- interesting enough, they talk about the-- the German Superman-- he was a small guy, maybe as tall-- a little bit-- maybe an inch taller than I am right now. And he gave us a speech, almost five minutes. And he had the nerve to tell us, I want you to know you are now in concentration camp Sachsenhausen. If you behave and you are disciplined and you follow the orders that you get and you work honestly and with effort, you will be treated nicely. You will get food. You will be-- if you are sick, we will take care of you. Just discipline and obedience-- that's all we ask from you. We said to ourselves, oh, my God. We heard so many terrible things about concentration camps. Because this was only '39. And I want you to know that tens of thousands, if not hundreds of thousands, of Germans were sent to concentration camps. All those that opposed the Nazis-- communists, socialists, Social Democrats, Jehovah Witnesses, homosexuals. And-- and when they came back-- some of them were released after a year or two. They were, so to speak, went through a process of re-education. The Nazis wanted to teach them how good it is to be a German citizen of the Third Reich and they were all wrong, and those that accepted that were released. And they were afraid to tell what is going on, but somehow it filtered through, and we knew that terrible things happened in concentration camps. And then in 1938, after the Kristallnacht, the German Jews-- over 25,000-- were sent to concentration at Dachau, Sachsenhausen, Buchenwald, and they came back and they told what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were scared to death. And here is this untersturmfuhrer tells us, if you obey the laws and you work and you are disciplined, nothing will happen to you. So-- I mean, we were afraid to talk to each other because we didn't-- we were not allowed to talk, but still we whispered. Maybe it's all propaganda. Maybe-- maybe we can live through the war here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody expected the war to last six years. We expected the-- the war would last a year, and then we will release. We will be deported. We'll be sent back to-- to Israel, to Palestine, what have you. 15 minutes after this SS officer told us, the bullies came in, about either six or eight SS guards-- so to speak, lower sergeants, corporals. They called it blockfuhrer, rapportfuhrer, all kinds of fuhrer. Everyone had the name 'fuhrer.' And they said, so in order that you get acquainted with the spirit of Sachsenhausen, we will make some sport. By the way, it was already October, and there was snow already. We-- around the-- the-- the entrance, there was-- they called it the appellplatz, a giant-- like a parade ground, where every morning they took roll call, where all the barracks had to march up in blocks and were counted and then they they reported to a rapportfuhrer that everybody's there. Every morning and every night they counted everybody. They want to know that nobody fled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the bullies come in. Let's make sports.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My God. I can't even tell you what-- what kind of sport this was. It was murder. They let old people on-- in the cold snow roll on, and they were trampling on the people. And-- and they had even arms with them, rifles, and they're beating it up. And this was going on at least for half an hour. I mean, it-- it's-- they called it sport. Everything was deliberately with deception called a name. Like-- like they called, for example, the-- the destruction of the Jews the Endlosung-- the solution, so to speak. They were beating us up. It was utterly unbelievable. And-- and you had to be very quick-- I was young-- to-- to evade. Whenever you saw a-- a-- an SS guard coming to you to beat you up, you rolled to the other side, and-- and-- to let him pick somebody else. As a result of this sport, there were, out of the 67 that we were, at least 15 that couldn't walk anymore and-- and three that were dead. They got heart attacks. They were beaten. They were elderly people. They were people 40, 50, 60 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got the first taste what really life in concentration camp is and-- and the lies that-- that Rudolf Hoss told us. We know that is the way a concentration camp and all the actions of the Nazis operate-- deception, deception, deception. They use words that have a general meaning, but to them it means something else altogether. It was just a cover-up for literally murdering us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now in Sachsenhausen, let me put it this way. They had no gas chambers there yet. It was early. They killed the Jews by chicanery, by hard work, and by giving very little food-- maybe 1,200 calories a day. We got twice a day a water soup that was dishwater soup, we got a quarter of a bread, and some coffee, and that's all. And there were no vitamins. And in no time, everybody got sick. We got abscesses all over, all over the body, because the-- the-- and-- and Jews were not-- there was a two blocks-- barracks-- they called this [NON-ENGLISH], they call this in German-- sick barracks, where Germans were treated. I don't know how well they were treated, but they got some medicine, primitive medicine. Jews were not allowed even to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about yourself. Did you ever get to the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I tell you-- I tell you what my luck was. I was very young. I was only 15. I looked 13. And somehow the German blockalteste and the kapos had a little bit-- call it mercy with me. I give an example. One, we always, the young people, volunteered to go to the central kitchen to pick up the barrels-- you know, the soup was-- was carried in barrels and-- and the bread-- because near the kitchen you could steal a-- a beet or a-- a tomato, or-- or maybe even a-- somebody stole you a piece of bread. We always volunteered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day the-- the chief, the SS hauptscharfuhrer of the kitchen, stood on the outside like this, like Superman, and he saw me and the other-- and the other youngsters. And he started saying in German, what is this here? Kindergarten? This is a concentration camp. And he caught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the smallest, the youngest one. As a matter of fact, I was the youngest in all of Sachsenhausen. In all the camp, there were 18,000 imprison-- mostly Germans, Poles, and what have you. The Jewish in Sachsenhausen, there were only 25,000 or 30,000 Jews, no more at the time. Then they deported them. Others came. And he called, come here! And I detected that this man is from Westphalia. He-- he was a very primitive man, not educated, and he spoke with a typical Westphalia [INAUDIBLE] slang. And I knew that, and I imitated it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, where are you from? The city of Gelsenkirchen has a nickname, [GERMAN]. And I said, I am from [GERMAN]. When he heard that-- he go-- so am I, he said. So he had some sympathy. He said, what are you doing here? What are you doing here? I said, I asked myself that question since I am in here. [LAUGHS] I was fresh. He said, don't be fresh. They'll beat you up, he said to me. And then he asked me, are you hungry? I said, I'm starving. Go, fill your belly. And he set me inside the kitchen to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of all the institutions that they had in the camp, the kitchen was strictly reserved for political prisoners. No German with a green wing-- angle that were criminals were-- weren't allowed there. And they were all young. They mostly were socialists or communists, well-educated, people who were students. Some were even young professors. And I made right away arrangement that every day we got a special extra barrel for the youth. They-- they had sympathy with young people, and they knew if anybody had a chance to survive this barbaric treatment in the camps were the young people. So to a degree the extra barrel saved our lives, because every 100 calories was decisive if you get sick or if you survive, or you get emaciated. After a while, you're just a-- a skeleton.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you can imagine a luck that this guy [LAUGHS] was from my hometown and he's feeling sorry for me. In fact, later on, he came-- he came to Auschwitz, too, later, [LAUGHS] and he recognized me. It's unbelievable. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so that was our life in Sachsenhausen. And then at the beginning, I worked as [GERMAN] in the barrack to clean, as a helper. Then there were always-- every day new instructions came from the kommandant of the camp. They need people to work. No more [GERMAN]. Let them do it after work, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your father and brother still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. In the same-- in the same barrack. But they were assigned to different work kommandos.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was assigned then to work-- there was a big factory that made bricks, special bricks, for high-- for high temperature. Were special bricks. They called this [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a big factory. And we marched out every day, worked there, and at night we marched back. Let's stop here, and we'll change tapes.
